content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


July 10, 2002


Julie Hayward Biggs, Esq.
Burke, Williams & Sorenson, LLP
3403 Tenth Street, Suite 300
Riverside, CA 92501

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-102

Dear Ms. Biggs:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note, our response is based on the facts
presented. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in its advice-giving capacity
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and our advice is applicable only to the extent
that facts provided to us are correct and that all of the material facts have been provided.

QUESTION

May Councilmember Brock participate in city council decisions concerning the
Ellwood Mesa and Devereux Slough Regional Open Space and Development Plan
("EDRP") where her property is within 500 feet of the project area?

CONCLUSION

Councilmember Brock may participate in city council decisions concerning the
Ellwood Mesa and Devereux Slough Regional Open Space and Development Plan so
long as 10 percent of the homeowners or property owners in the city are affected in
substantially the same manner.

FACTS

The City of Goleta is located in Santa Barbara County, and was incorporated on
February 1, 2002. Prior to incorporation, the County of Santa Barbara, the University of

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-02-102
Page No. 2

California at Santa Barbara ("UCSB"), and the Santa Barbara Development Partnership,
Inc. ("Developer") were engaged in negotiations and proceedings to create a regional
plan for certain coastal areas extending from UCSB's Coal Point Ecological Reserve to a
point north and west of UCSB within the city boundaries of Goleta. The proposed
regional plan, known as the "Ellwood Mesa and Devereux Slough Regional Open Space
and Development Plan ("EDRP"), would lead to the transfer of proposed dwelling units
within an existing specific plan for the Ellwood Beach-Santa Barbara Shores from its
currently proposed location to a city owned park ("Park") within the specific plan area.
In addition, the EDRP would transfer some dwelling units from property located in the
County of Santa Barbara owned by UCSB to property that is adjacent to existing
residential neighborhoods within the City of Goleta.

A primary goal of the EDRP is to preserve critical habitat for the Monarch
butterfly and other sensitive coastal species while allowing residential development to
proceed in areas where there is a lesser environmental impact. Under the EDRP, the
habitat preserve would become a protected open space area accessible to the public with
limited public facilities and parking.

The EDRP covers an area of approximately 993 acres that stretches along 2.25
miles of coastline. This encompasses virtually all undeveloped coastline property within
the boundaries of the new City of Goleta. Now that the city has become a separate
governmental entity, it has been asked to join with the county, the UCSB, and the
Developer in the processing of the EDRP.

Adoption of the EDRP will involve governmental decisions by the County of
Santa Barbara, UCSB and the City of Goleta, as well as agreement by the Developer. It
is difficult to predict what economic effect, if any, the proposed EDRP will have on
adjacent or regional properties.

Based on information provided by the County of Santa Barbara Planning and
Development Department, which was derived from the 2000 census, there are
approximately 28,000 residents in the City of Goleta. According to that information,
there are approximately 5,635 households in the City of Goleta that are single family
dwellings. Of those, 1,166 households (20.7%) are located within a 500 foot radius of
the boundaries of the EDRP area and approximately 1,816 households (32.2%) are within
a 1,000 foot radius of the boundaries of the EDRP area.

One member of the Goleta City Council, Cynthia Brook, has a community
property ownership interest of more than $2,000 in a home owned by her husband that is
within a 500-foot radius of the boundary of a portion of the proposed EDRP area. This
single-family dwelling is Councilmember Brook's primary residence. Councilmember
Brook owns no other property within the jurisdiction.

On June 28, 2002, you faxed additional numerical information regarding the City
of Goleta. According to that information, there are approximately 5,905 single family
type residential properties in the City of Goleta. The total number of single family type

File No. A-02-102
Page No. 3

residential properties within 500 feet of the boundaries of the EDRP was given as 1,173.
The total number of property owners in the City of Goleta was given as 8,911. On that
same day, in a telephone conversation you noted that Councilmember Brock would be
voting on the relocation of the butterfly territory. In the future, Councilmember Brock
may possibly vote on a tract map for the redevelopment project as well, but this decision
is limited to relocating a pre-approved subdivision development site. In essence, this will
be a vote regarding a land swap.

You also told us that both the current development site and the proposed
relocation site are approximately the same distance from the council member's residence
(500 feet). Her house is at the outer edge of the 500-foot boundary in reference to either
site. In addition, her house is located approximately two streets from the south site
boundary and four streets from the west site boundary. The council member's house is
also identical to all the other houses in the subdivision.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted a standard, eight-
step analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in
a given governmental decision as follows:

(1) Determine whether the individual is a public official, within the meaning of
the Act.

(2) Determine whether the public official will be making, participating in making,
or using or attempting to use his/her official position to influence a government decision.

(3) Identify the public official's economic interests.²

(4) For each of the public official's economic interests, determine whether that
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision.

(5) Determine the applicable materiality standard for each economic interest.

(6) Determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision
will have a material financial effect ... on each economic interest identified.

_______________
² You note that Councilmember Brock has a community property interest in property owned by
her husband. Please note that the statutory economic interests of the council member include "indirect"
interests. An indirect interest means any interest in real property owned by the spouse of an official or by a
member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business
entity or trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly, indirectly,
or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater. (§ 87103.) This definition is not dependent on community
property law.

File No. A-02-102
Page No. 4

(7) Determine if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect is distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.

(8) Determine if the public official's participation is legally required despite the
conflict of interest.

Your question concerns only the seventh step in the standard conflict-of-interest
analysis. You ask whether the "public generally" exception applies to your facts.

Regulation 18707.1(b)³ provides in pertinent part:

"(1) Significant Segment. The governmental
decision will affect a 'significant segment' of the public
generally if any of the following are affected as set forth
below:

¶...¶

"(B) Real Property. For decisions that affect a
public official's real property interest, the decision also
affects:

"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or
all homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency
or the district the official represents; or

"(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency.

¶...¶

"(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The
governmental decision will affect a public official's
economic interest in substantially the same manner as it
will affect the significant segment identified in subdivision
(b)(1) of this regulation."

You note that there are approximately 28,000 residents in the City of Goleta.
There are approximately 5,905 single family type residential properties in the City of
Goleta and 8,911 total property owners. Of those total property owners, 1,173 single
family type households are located within 500 feet of the boundaries of the EDRP area.
The test in regulation 18707.1 uses homeowners or property owners. There are
approximately 13.2 percent (1173/8911) of the total property owners within the 500-foot

³ In your letter you cite to regulation 18707.9. However, this section is intended to be applied in
the context of rental property. Since the council member's property is her primary residence we apply the
general test which has substantially the same thresholds as regulation 18707.9.

File No. A-02-102
Page No. 5

boundary and 19.9 percent (1173/5905) of single family type residential properties within
the 500-foot boundary.

In addition, you note that the relocation in question will have a minimal effect on
the council member's property since both locations are approximately the same distance
from her property. You also state that this effect will be shared in substantially the same
manner with all those property owners near the site in question. If, in fact, it is true that
the homeowners in the area will be affected in substantially the same manner as
Councilmember Brock's property, then the exception would apply and Councilmember
Brock could participate. Councilmember Brock ultimately must make this determination,
since the Commission does not act as a finder of fact.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: [signature]
Galena West
Counsel, Legal Division

Ellwood Mesa and Devereux Slough Regional Open Space and Development Plan so
long as 10 percent of the homeowners or property owners in the city are affected in
substantially the same manner.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember Brock participate in city council decisions concerning the
Ellwood Mesa and Devereux Slough Regional Open Space and Development Plan
("EDIU"') where her property is within 500 feet of the project area?

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Brock may participate in city council decisions concerning the
Ellwood Mesa and Devereux Slough Regional Open Space and Development Plan so
long as 10 percent of the homeowners or property owners in the city are affected in
substantially the same manner.
